items.title: ‘A missed opportunity’: Payers lash out against surprise billing final rule - Healthcare Dive
items.reason: This article directly addresses payer reactions to the No Surprises Act surprise billing final rule, which is a core component of the Act's implementation and rulemaking process that affects revenue-cycle operations.
items.summary: Healthcare payers have publicly criticized the final surprise billing rule issued under the No Surprises Act, characterizing it as a missed opportunity. The article covers payer feedback on the federal rulemaking that implements balance-billing protections and dispute resolution requirements, indicating industry disagreement with regulatory approach. Payer concerns about the rule's implementation directly affect revenue-cycle teams managing surprise billing compliance, claims processing, and Independent Dispute Resolution procedures. Understanding payer-specific critiques of the rule helps revenue-cycle teams anticipate enforcement challenges and operational impacts from the regulatory framework.

also.title: AHIP cites 'provider-driven' abuse in IDR process - Healthcare Finance News

items.title: Judge Tosses Aetna’s Case Claiming Medivacs Abuse Surprise Billing - Bloomberg Law News
items.reason: This litigation involves a dispute over surprise billing protections and balance-billing practices under the No Surprises Act framework, making it directly relevant to NSA litigation and enforcement.
items.summary: A judge dismissed Aetna's lawsuit alleging that air ambulance (medivac) providers were exploiting surprise billing loopholes to charge patients without proper NSA protections. The case centered on whether medivac services fall under the No Surprises Act's balance-billing safeguards and whether providers were circumventing these protections. The dismissal affects how insurers can challenge provider billing practices related to emergency air transport services. This outcome matters for revenue-cycle teams managing surprise billing disputes, as it clarifies judicial interpretation of NSA coverage scope and insurers' standing to challenge such practices.
